dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 for a diabetic emergency at a residence in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Taylor and this is Jordan; we're EMTs. What happened here?
My father's diabetic and became really unresponsive. His BGL was 43 before you came.
Mr. Smith, can you open your eyes and look at us?
Hmm… Yeah.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your name?
John... John Smith.
Mr. Smith, can you squeeze my finger and tell me where we are?
Richmond, I think.
Airway is patent and self-maintained; no signs of obstruction.
Your breathing doesn't seem labored. Any trouble with it?
No, just tired.
Breathing is non-labored with increased respiratory effort noted on inspection. No accessory muscle use at this time.
Let me check your pulse now. Anything numb or tingly on your limbs?
Pulse is 56, smooth, and regular.
Respirations are 18 per minute and adequate in depth.
Blood pressure is 158 over 76. Radial pulse is weak but regular.
Your hands are cold, but the rest of you feels warm and dry.
Mr. Smith, any new symptoms or changes in your body lately?
He was fine until minutes before we called. Then he got unresponsive.
What medications is he taking daily?
Metoprolol, Amlodipine, Atorvastatin.
Does he have a history of diabetes?
Yes, insulin-dependent.
What was the last thing he ate or drank?
He had dinner a couple of hours ago. We don't know when he had lunch.
Tell me exactly what happened before the call.
He started acting very strange. I gave him some sugar, but he didn't react much.
Mr. Smith, are you experiencing any pain in your chest, arms, or back?
No pain at all.
Let's test your blood sugar now. This may sting a little.
Blood glucose is 40 mg/dL. We need to start D10 immediately.
You feel dry and warm with the exception of your hands, which are cold. Are there any rashes or bruises?
No, I think I'm fine.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light before treatment with dextrose.
No, feels okay.
I'm going to check your neck now. Any stiffness or pain?
No stiffness or nuchal rigidity.
Let me listen to your chest. Breathe in deeply.
Breathe in and hold for three seconds.
You showed some accessory muscle use with breathing. Any pain with inspiration or expiration?
Increased respiratory effort on both sides-any pain with breathing?
Let's check your oxygen level. Just breathe normally.
Oxygen saturation is 99 percent. That's normal.
I need to place a cardiac monitor on you. Any discomfort?
ECG shows sinus bradycardia with occasional premature atrial contractions.
We are starting 4-lead monitoring per protocol to track any arrhythmias.
Any injuries or pain in your arms or legs?
No, I feel fine now.
Peripheral pulses can be detected but are weak at this time. No deformity or angulation noted.
Let's do a FAST exam to screen for stroke. Can you show me both of your feet and raise your arms?
Yes, I can do that.
No signs of facial drooping, arm weakness, or speech difficulties. Exam is negative for stroke.
Starting IV based on the low blood sugar. Protocol for diabetic hypoglycemia is in place.
Establishing IV access in left arm with 20G catheter at the extremity vein.
Performing two-person medication cross-check before administering dextrose.
Administering 250 mL of D10 now to correct the hypoglycemic state.
We'll recheck your BGL soon. Any change in how you feel?
He's becoming more alert. GCS is 12 (E3;V4;M5) on initial administration, showing altered mental status.
Altered mental status was noted initially, consistent with hypoglycemia.
Blood sugar is now 125 mg/dL. We'll check it again in 10 minutes.
Radial pulse is now strong and regular. Still showing sinus bradycardia with occasional PACs.
Now administering the final 250 mL of D10 IV.
GCS improved to 15 (E4, V5, M6). Now oriented to self and environment.
Prior to administration of D10, his mental status was AAOx1/4. He responded to verbal cues but wasn't fully orientated.
After the first dose of D10, his mental status improved to AAOx4/4, and he became fully orientated.
BGL is now 157 mg/dL after full administration. Pain remains at 0. He feels good.
Performing a 12-lead ECG with the patient alert and stable.
12-lead ECG identified minimal initial anterior vector with abnormal Q-R patterns in lead V2.
There are ST-T wave abnormalities seen in I/aVL/V5/V6, with moderate evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy.
Also, T-wave inversion in II/aVF at -0.1+ mV consistent with possible inferior ST-T wave ischemia.
Additional findings include a second-degree AV block, Mobitz type II, and incomplete right bundle branch block.
No ST elevation present to suggest acute myocardial infarction based on the 12-lead.
IV bolus is complete. No need to administer further D10. Site is clean and bleeding minimal.
Mr. Smith, would you like to stay at home or come to the hospital with us?
I think I'm fine. I'd prefer to stay at home.
Alright, your family will stay with you. Watch for any changes in mental status.
We've advised your family to monitor closely and call us if things change.
EMS report will be filed in the system in case you need hospital records later.
If you feel anything off-like confusion or chest pain-call 911 again.
Cardiovascular assessment found a weak but regular radial pulse before treatment and a strong and regular pulse after administration of D10.
IV access was via the left upper extremity vein at the antecubital fossa, confirmed patency by 10mL flush.
12-lead EKG showed moderate evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy, consistent with chronic hypertension.
Premature atrial contractions were noted on EKG; no other life-threatening arrhythmias observed.
Abnormal Q-R complexes in lead V2 were observed during the 12-lead EKG.
No ST elevation present to suggest acute myocardial infarction based on the 12-lead.
ST-T wave abnormalities were seen in multiple leads and may indicate possible ischemia.
